summary:
Tequendama is a rock shelter site near the town of Soacha in the Cundinamarca Department of Colombia, located in the Eastern Cordillera highlands at an elevation of approximately 2,570 meters above sea level. It is one of the most important early human occupation sites in northern South America, with radiocarbon dates extending from approximately 10,500 to 2,500 BCE.

Excavated in the late 1960s and 1970s, Tequendama yielded stratified cultural deposits documenting the lives of early South American hunter-gatherers in the Andean highlands. The site's long sequence documents shifts in technology, diet, and material culture across thousands of years, including human burials and associated artifacts. The inhabitants of Tequendama exploited high-altitude resources including deer, guinea pigs, and Andean camelids (ancestors of llama and alpaca), alongside plant foods. Tequendama is part of a broader pattern of early human occupation of Andean highland zones in Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru during the early Holocene, reflecting the rapid dispersal of Paleo-Indian populations through South America following the initial colonization of the continent documented at sites such as [[event:1285|Monte Verde]] in Chile.
